questions.question: What defines a TIA (transient ischemic attack)?
options: A. A stroke that leaves permanent deficits
B. Stroke-like neurologic deficit that resolves completely, classically within minutes to an hour
C. A minor headache with no neurologic findings
D. A seizure followed by confusion
questions.rationale: A TIA produces stroke-like focal neurologic deficit that resolves completely without lasting tissue injury, classically within minutes to an hour.

questions.question: Why is a TIA treated with the same field urgency as an ongoing stroke?
options: A. It cannot be reliably distinguished from an evolving stroke in the field, and carries short-term risk of a completed stroke
B. TIAs are more dangerous than strokes
C. TIA is simply an outdated term for stroke
D. There is no urgency difference because TIAs are never serious
questions.rationale: Because a TIA can't be reliably distinguished from an evolving stroke in the field, and TIA patients face a meaningful short-term risk of a subsequent completed stroke, both are managed with the same urgency.

questions.question: What are the two broad categories of stroke?
options: A. Ischemic and hemorrhagic
B. Acute and chronic
C. Central and peripheral
D. Primary and secondary
questions.rationale: Stroke is broadly categorized as ischemic (obstruction of blood flow) or hemorrhagic (bleeding into/around brain tissue).

questions.question: Can ischemic and hemorrhagic stroke be reliably distinguished in the field by symptoms alone?
options: A. Yes, symptoms are always distinct
B. No — both cause focal neurologic deficits; imaging is needed to distinguish them
C. Only hemorrhagic stroke causes a facial droop
D. Only ischemic stroke causes speech abnormality
questions.rationale: Field symptoms cannot reliably distinguish the two stroke types; both produce focal neurologic deficits, and hospital imaging is required to differentiate them.

questions.question: What three findings does the Cincinnati Prehospital Stroke Scale (CPSS) assess?
options: A. Facial droop, arm drift, speech abnormality
B. Blood pressure, heart rate, respiratory rate
C. Pupil size, gag reflex, grip strength
D. Gaze deviation, neglect, ataxia
questions.rationale: CPSS checks facial droop, arm drift, and speech abnormality as its three core findings.

questions.question: What is the purpose of an LVO-screening scale like RACE or FAST-ED?
options: A. To diagnose the exact stroke type in the field
B. To add sensitivity for detecting a large-vessel occlusion, which may inform destination decisions
C. To replace blood glucose checks
D. To determine the patient's code status
questions.rationale: LVO-screening scales add sensitivity for detecting large-vessel occlusion strokes, which may influence a destination decision toward an endovascular-capable center per local protocol.

questions.question: What does FAST-ED add to the standard FAST components?
options: A. Eye deviation and denial/neglect
B. Blood glucose and temperature
C. Pupil reactivity
D. Grip strength bilaterally
questions.rationale: FAST-ED extends FAST by adding eye (gaze) deviation and denial/neglect to improve LVO detection sensitivity.

questions.question: Which findings raise suspicion for a large-vessel occlusion (LVO) stroke?
options: A. Isolated mild facial droop only
B. Gaze deviation, severe combined face+arm weakness, and neglect of the deficit
C. A resolved speech abnormality with normal exam otherwise
D. Isolated numbness without weakness
questions.rationale: Gaze deviation, severe unilateral face+arm weakness together, and neglect/denial of the deficit are findings that raise LVO suspicion beyond a basic FAST-positive screen.

questions.question: Why should blood glucose be checked in a suspected stroke patient?
options: A. Hypoglycemia is a well-known, rapidly correctable stroke mimic
B. Glucose has no relevance to neurologic presentations
C. It replaces the need for a stroke scale
D. It is only relevant in diabetic patients
questions.rationale: Hypoglycemia can mimic a focal neurologic deficit and is rapidly correctable, making a glucose check a high-value field step in suspected stroke.

questions.question: Why is documenting the last-known-well time so important?
options: A. It drives eligibility for time-sensitive hospital treatments
B. It is only used for billing purposes
C. It has no bearing on hospital treatment decisions
D. It replaces the need for a stroke scale
questions.rationale: Last-known-well time is a key driver of eligibility for time-sensitive interventions at the receiving hospital, making precise documentation a high priority.

questions.question: What is a 'stroke alert'?
options: A. A patient-initiated emergency call
B. Early hospital notification of a suspected stroke patient so the receiving facility can prepare
C. A specific stroke medication
D. A type of cardiac monitor alarm
questions.rationale: A stroke alert is early notification to the receiving hospital, allowing the stroke team to prepare before patient arrival.

questions.question: Should transport be delayed to perform a full, extensive neurologic exam on a suspected stroke patient?
options: A. Yes, always complete a full exam before transport
B. No — use a rapid validated screening scale and prioritize rapid transport, since time is central to stroke outcome
C. Only if the patient requests it
D. Only for hemorrhagic strokes
questions.rationale: Time is the central variable in stroke outcomes, so field care uses a rapid screening scale rather than delaying transport for an extensive exam.

questions.question: A patient had left-sided facial droop and slurred speech that fully resolved 20 minutes before EMS arrival. How should this be treated?
options: A. As a non-urgent complaint since symptoms resolved
B. With the same urgency as an active stroke, given the possibility of TIA and risk of a subsequent stroke
C. Only urgent if the patient becomes symptomatic again
D. As a psychiatric complaint given full resolution
questions.rationale: A resolved deficit consistent with TIA still carries meaningful short-term stroke risk and should be treated with full stroke-protocol urgency.

questions.question: A stroke patient has gaze deviation to the right, severe right face and arm weakness, and does not acknowledge the weak arm. What should this presentation raise suspicion for?
options: A. A simple TIA with no further workup needed
B. Possible large-vessel occlusion (LVO) stroke, potentially warranting an LVO-screening-based destination decision
C. A hypoglycemic event with no stroke involvement
D. A behavioral/psychiatric emergency
questions.rationale: Gaze deviation, combined severe face+arm weakness, and neglect of the deficit are classic LVO-suspicious findings that may change the transport destination decision per local protocol.

questions.question: Which guideline is the authoritative current source for stroke management referenced in this course?
options: A. The 2026 AHA/ASA Guideline for the Early Management of Patients With Acute Ischemic Stroke
B. GINA 2025
C. DSM-5-TR
D. ADA Standards of Care 2026
questions.rationale: The 2026 AHA/ASA Guideline for the Early Management of Patients With Acute Ischemic Stroke is the current authoritative source, replacing the 2018/2019 guideline.
